title: How to Use Intuition When Making Career Decisions
meta_description: Learn to trust your gut when choosing a career path. This article shows how to recognize intuitive signals and use them for confident decisions.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Career Decisions
og_description: Learn to trust your gut when choosing a career path. This article shows how to recognize intuitive signals and use them for confident decisions.
body:
When Your Job Makes You Want to Crawl Out of Your Skin

You know that feeling. You're sitting in another meeting, staring at the clock, and something in your chest is screaming. Not loud. Not dramatic. It's more like a low hum that won't stop. Like your whole body is saying, "This isn't it. This isn't you." But you've got bills. You've got a resume that looks good on paper. You've got people telling you to be grateful.

So you stay. And the hum gets louder. And you start to wonder if something's broken in you.

Nothing's broken. You're just sensing things that most people have learned to ignore. That hum? That's your intuition. It's been talking to you your whole life, but nobody taught you how to listen to it, especially not when it comes to something as scary as your career.

I'm Kalesh, and I've worked with hundreds of people who feel things too much. People whose nervous systems pick up on stuff that isn't in the job description. People who walk into an office and know, within five minutes, that something's off. People who've been told they're "too sensitive" for the corporate world. Here's the truth: you're not too sensitive. You're calibrated for a wider range of information than most. And that sensitivity? It's your best career tool. You just need to learn how to use it without losing your mind.

Why Your Gut Is Smarter Than Your Resume

Let's get one thing straight. Your intuition isn't some woo-woo magic that floats in from nowhere. It's your nervous system processing information that your conscious mind hasn't caught up with yet. You've been in enough rooms, enough meetings, enough jobs that your body has a library of data it's constantly comparing against. When something feels wrong, it's because your system has already flagged it. You just haven't learned to trust that flag.

The career you're supposed to be in doesn't just make sense on paper - it makes sense in your bones. If your body is fighting your job every day, your resume is lying to you.

Think about the last time you ignored a gut feeling about a job. Maybe you took the position because the salary was good, even though something in your stomach turned during the interview. Maybe you stayed at a company for years because it looked stable, even though you felt drained every Sunday night. How did that work out for you? Probably not great. Right?!

Your intuition isn't asking you to quit your job tomorrow and move to a monastery. It's asking you to pay attention. To notice what your body does when you think about different career paths. To stop treating your career like a logic puzzle and start treating it like a relationship. Because that's what it is. A relationship between you and your work. And if that relationship is toxic, no amount of salary or prestige is going to fix it.

The Three Types of Intuitive Signals You're Already Getting

Most people think intuition is a single thing. A flash. A hunch. A voice. But it's not that simple. Your intuition talks to you through three main channels, and if you're a sensitive person, you're probably getting all three at once. That's why it feels overwhelming. Let me break them down.

1. Body Signals (The Physical Channel)

This is the most reliable one, and the one most people ignore. Your body doesn't lie. It can't. When you're considering a career move, pay attention to what your body does. Not your thoughts. Not your worries. Your actual physical sensations.

When you think about taking that new job, does your chest tighten or open? When you imagine staying at your current company for another year, does your stomach clench or relax? When you picture yourself in that new role, do your shoulders drop or do they go up toward your ears?

These aren't random. They're data. Your body is giving you a readout of what's aligned with your deepest self and what isn't. The problem is, most of us have been trained to override these signals. We tell ourselves we're being dramatic. We tell ourselves to be logical. But logic without body data is just guessing. And guessing is how you end up in a job that makes you want to crawl out of your skin.

2. Emotional Signals (The Feeling Channel)

Your emotions are not your enemy. I know you've been told they are. You've been told you're "too emotional" or "too sensitive." But here's the thing: your emotions are your nervous system's way of telling you what's important. They're not random noise. They're signals about what matters to you.

When you think about a career decision, notice what emotions come up. Not the surface ones - not the fear of what other people will think or the anxiety about money. Go deeper. What's underneath that? Is there excitement buried under the fear? Is there grief under the anger? Is there relief under the guilt?

Your emotions are telling you what you actually want. The trick is learning to separate your authentic emotions from the ones you've been trained to feel. Your mother's anxiety about job security is not your intuition. Your partner's fear about you changing is not your intuition. Your own deep, quiet knowing? That's the one to listen to. Does that land?

3. Synchronicity Signals (The External Channel)

This one's a little weirder, but stick with me. Have you ever noticed that when you're on the right path, things start lining up? You think about a new career, and suddenly you run into someone who works in that field. You consider going back to school, and a program announcement shows up in your feed. You wonder if you should start your own business, and an unexpected check arrives in the mail.

These aren't coincidences. Or rather, they are - but they're meaningful coincidences. Your intuition works through the external world too. When you're aligned with your path, the universe tends to cooperate. Not because magic is making it happen, but because you're paying attention. You're open. You're looking for signs. And when you're looking, you find them.

If you're constantly hitting dead ends, if every door seems to slam in your face, that's also a signal. Not a punishment. Just information. Maybe that path isn't yours. Maybe you need to turn around and try a different door.

How to Actually Use This Stuff (The Practical Part)

Okay, so you're getting signals. Now what? How do you actually use intuition to make a career decision without quitting your job in a panic and ending up broke and confused? Here's the process I teach my clients. It's not complicated, but it takes practice.

Step 1: Create Space to Listen

You can't hear your intuition if you're constantly drowning it out with noise. I don't just mean external noise - podcasts, notifications, other people's opinions. I mean internal noise. The constant loop of worry, planning, and self-doubt. You need to create space to actually feel what you're feeling.

Here's a simple practice. Set a timer for ten minutes. Sit somewhere quiet. Close your eyes. And ask yourself one question: "If I had no fear and no constraints, what would I want to do with my work?" Don't try to answer it. Just sit with the question. Notice what comes up. A memory. A feeling. A image. That's your intuition talking. Don't judge it. Don't analyze it. Just let it be there.

Do this every day for a week. You'll be surprised what surfaces when you stop trying to force an answer.

Step 2: Gather Data Without Deciding

This is where most people mess up. They get a hit of intuition and immediately try to act on it. "I felt something about graphic design, so I'm quitting my accounting job tomorrow." No. That's not intuition. That's impulse. Intuition is patient. It gives you a direction, not a command.

Instead of deciding, gather data. If you feel pulled toward a new career, spend a week just researching it. Read about it. Talk to people who do it. Notice how your body feels as you learn more. Does the excitement grow? Does it fade? Does something else come up?

If you feel like you need to leave your current job, don't quit yet. Start updating your resume. Look at job postings. Imagine yourself in other roles. See how your system responds. You're not committing to anything. You're just collecting information. Your intuition will clarify as you gather more data.

Step 3: Test the Waters with Small Actions

This is the secret to using intuition without wrecking your life. Instead of making a huge leap, take a small step. Your intuition will tell you whether that step feels right or wrong. Then take another small step. And another. Before you know it, you've built a path without ever making a terrifying, all-in bet.

For example, if you're considering a career change to coaching, don't quit your job and hang a shingle. Offer to coach one friend for free. See how it feels. If it lights you up, do it again. If it drains you, try something else. Your intuition will guide you step by step, but only if you're actually taking steps.

For a deeper dive into this process, I highly recommend The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's not specifically about career decisions, but it's one of the best books I've found on learning to trust yourself - which is what intuition is really about. See on Amazon (paid link).

What to Do When Your Intuition Contradicts Your Logic

This is the hard part. You've done your research. You've made a spreadsheet. The logical choice is clear. But your gut is saying something different. What do you do?

First, don't ignore the logic. Your rational mind is not your enemy. It's there for a reason. It's good at assessing risk, calculating probabilities, and keeping you safe. But it's not good at knowing what will make you feel alive. That's your intuition's job.

Here's how I think about it. Your logic is the map. Your intuition is the compass. The map shows you the terrain - the obstacles, the distances, the options. But the compass tells you which direction to go. You need both. If you only follow the map, you'll end up somewhere safe but dead inside. If you only follow the compass, you'll end up lost and broke. You need the map to work through, but you need the compass to know where you're going.

When logic and intuition conflict, don't pick one. Ask yourself: "What would it take for both to be true?" Maybe the logical path is the one that pays the bills while you build the intuitive path on the side. Maybe the intuitive path is actually more logical than you think - you just haven't done the math yet. Maybe you need to wait. Maybe you need to leap. But don't force a choice between your head and your gut. They're both part of you. They need to work together.

If you want to understand this dynamic better, check out Thinking, Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman. It's a dense read, but it'll show you exactly how your brain works - and why your intuition is often smarter than your logic. See on Amazon (paid link).

When You're Stuck in Indecision (And Can't Feel Anything)

Sometimes you don't get a clear signal. Sometimes you feel numb. Sometimes you've been ignoring your intuition for so long that it's stopped talking. Or maybe you're so anxious that you can't tell the difference between fear and intuition.

If you're stuck, stop trying to figure it out. Your brain is in a loop, and it's not going to solve itself by thinking harder. You need to get out of your head and into your body. Move. Go for a walk. Take a shower. Dance. Do something physical that doesn't require decision-making. Your intuition lives in your body, not your brain. If you can't feel your body, you can't feel your intuition.

Another trick: pretend you've already made the decision. For one day, act as if you chose Option A. Notice how you feel. The next day, act as if you chose Option B. Notice the difference. Your body will tell you which one feels more true, even if your mind is still spinning.

And if you're really stuck, get some external support. A good coach or therapist can help you untangle the knots. Sometimes you need someone else to hold the space while you figure out what you actually want. There's no shame in that. We're not meant to do this alone.

The Fear Factor (And How to Work With It)

Let's talk about fear, because it's the biggest blocker for most people. You get a clear intuitive hit about a career move, and then fear shows up. "What if I fail? What if I can't pay my bills? What if everyone thinks I'm crazy?" And suddenly you're paralyzed.

Here's what I want you to understand. Fear is not the opposite of intuition. Fear is the gatekeeper. It's the thing that stands between you and the life you're meant to live. Your intuition shows you the door. Fear shows you the lock. And your job is to learn how to open up it.

Not by ignoring fear. Not by pretending it doesn't exist. But by recognizing it for what it is: a biological response to the unknown. Fear doesn't mean you're on the wrong path. Most of the time, it means you're on the right one. If you weren't scared, you wouldn't be growing.

So when fear shows up, don't fight it. Don't try to make it go away. Just notice it. "Oh, there's fear. It's doing its thing." And then take the next small step anyway. Fear will eventually settle down once it realizes you're not going to die. It just needs time to adjust.

For a practical guide to working with fear, I recommend The Big Leap by Gay Hendricks. It's all about why we self-sabotage when we're about to make a big positive change - and how to stop doing it. See on Amazon (paid link).

Trusting Yourself When No One Else Does

This is the hardest part. You'll make a decision based on your intuition, and people will question you. Your parents will worry. Your friends will think you're making a mistake. Your colleagues will tell you you're throwing away your career. And you'll doubt yourself.

That's normal. You're human. But here's the thing: other people can't feel what you feel. They don't have access to your internal data. They're looking at your life from the outside, using their own fears and assumptions. Their advice is about them, not you.

You are the only person who has to live with the consequences of your career decisions. Not your mother. Not your partner. Not your boss. You. So you have to learn to trust yourself, even when it feels lonely. Even when it feels scary. Even when you're not sure.

Start small. Trust your intuition about what to eat for lunch. Trust it about which route to take to work. Build the muscle of self-trust on small things, and it'll be there when you need it for the big things. Know what I mean?

One more book that helped me with this: Untamed by Glennon Doyle. It's about learning to trust your own knowing, even when the world tells you otherwise. See on Amazon (paid link).

The Bottom Line

Your intuition is not a luxury. It's not a nice-to-have. It's your internal guidance system, and it's been trying to talk to you your whole life. The question is whether you're ready to listen.

You don't have to make a perfect decision. You don't have to know everything upfront. You just have to take the next right step, based on what you know right now. And then the next one. And the next one. Your intuition will guide you, but only if you're willing to move.

So here's my invitation to you. Stop treating your career like a math problem. Start treating it like a conversation with your deepest self. Listen to your body. Trust your feelings. Pay attention to the signs. And when you get a clear hit, take a step - even a small one - in that direction.

You've got this. You've always had it. You just forgot.

If you want to go deeper on this, I've put together a short assessment that can help you figure out where you are in your career journey and what your next step might be. Take the assessment here - it's free, it takes about ten minutes, and it might give you the clarity you've been looking for.
